Dealing with all this fake stuff, this garbage information, it ain't about getting caught up in every little drama; it’s about keeping your head straight, like checking your pressure gauge before you start a job. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend roughly a few times weekly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: Look, when you're out there doing the work, whether it's wrestling with a busted main line or trying to get a decent shot of a sunset while traveling, you learn pretty quick that not everything you see is what it seems. Social media is the same racket, just with more shiny filters. Dealing with all this fake stuff, this garbage information, it ain't about getting caught up in every little drama; it’s about keeping your head straight, like checking your pressure gauge before you start a job. Since I don't spend hours glued to my phone, I’ve learned to be suspicious, not angry. When I see something online, especially something that makes my stomach clench or gets really emotional, my first move is to slow down. I treat it like a dodgy pipe connection—you don't just slap tape on it and call it fixed. I check the source. Who is saying this? Are they some random account with a profile picture stolen from a stock photo, or is it a place I generally trust? If it sounds too wild, too perfect, or just plain crazy, I treat it like a red flag on a construction site. I don't share it. I don't argue with it in the comments. Instead, I look for another angle, maybe Google the claim, or see if any of the folks I know who are actually sharp about these things have seen it. It’s a bit like when I’m learning a new knot for climbing; you don't just trust the guy who shouted the instructions; you check the diagram and test the knot yourself. The goal isn't to be a digital detective all the time, but to build a little mental firewall. If it doesn't add value to my life—if it ain't helpful, true, or something I genuinely need to know—then it's just noise. And noise is just wasted energy, which, frankly, I don't have when I've got a week's worth of plumbing calls waiting for me. A little healthy skepticism goes a long way.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a pragmatic, low-energy, moderately agreeable man (Plumber, low conscientiousness/extraversion, moderate agreeableness) who is curious but not deeply immersed in social media. The tone must be grounded, practical, and skeptical, reflecting his blue-collar, hands-on background. The response should use analogies from his life (plumbing, climbing, physical work) to explain the abstract concept of digital verification, aligning with his low openness score (preferring concrete reality over abstract theory).
